summary: The post explores how to symbolically represent computations that go beyond simple tree structures, proposing a bipartite directed acyclic graph (BiDAG) as a more general data structure. Rocklin introduces the Computation base type with inputs and outputs tuples, and a CompositeComputation type that collects multiple computations together. The approach emphasizes immutability and mathematical attributes over performance, with a deliberate separation between mathematics-based optimizations and infrastructure-based optimizations like inplace operations. This work is motivated by translating matrix expressions into BLAS/LAPACK operations, where mathematical pattern matching and inplace computation goals create tension that's resolved by handling them in separate phases.
key_points: Trees are too restrictive for representing computations with multiple outputs, necessitating bipartite directed acyclic graphs (BiDAGs)
The Computation type provides inputs/outputs tuples instead of the standard args used for trees, enabling more flexible composition
Variables are assumed immutable and unique, defined entirely by their mathematical meaning rather than computational attributes
Inplace operations and copies require an explicit transition from mathematics-based to infrastructure-based optimization by pairing mathematical expressions with computational variables
The separation of mathematical pattern matching from inplace optimization allows solving complex matrix expression translation problems in stages
money_quotes: While trees are a convenient data structure they are also very restrictive.
A DAG is the next most restrictive graph subtype. In some sense this is the smallest generalization we can make.
All variables are assumed immutable and unique. The intention is that variables should be entirely defined by their mathematical meaning.
This approach has a focus on immutability and mathematical attributes rather than performance and computational attributes.
We make an explicit separation between mathematics-based optimizations and infrastructure-based optimizations (like inplace).
Inplace computations provide higher performance at the cost of memory safety.
These two goals of mathematical pattern matching and inplace computations are challenging to solve simultaneously for non-trivial expressions.
key_insight: Separating mathematical semantics from computational infrastructure (like inplace operations) allows complex optimization problems to be solved in tractable stages rather than all at once.
